title: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
author: Michael Lockwood
orientation: Reacting to the Supreme Court's June 2020 decision striking down Louisiana's abortion clinic admitting-privileges law, Lockwood argues the ruling proves the futility of incremental pro-life regulation and calls for state magistrates to defy the Court and abolish abortion outright as murder.
audiences: pro-life advocate frustrated with Supreme Court losses
constitutional conservative weighing precedent vs. text
state legislator considering abolition vs. regulation

questions.question: What did the Supreme Court strike down in this June 2020 decision, and how narrow was the ruling?
questions.audience: pro-life advocate frustrated with Supreme Court losses
questions.answer: The Court issued a 5-4 ruling against a Louisiana law that would have required abortion clinics to maintain hospital admitting privileges within 30 miles. The law was a modest health regulation, not a ban, yet still failed at the Court.
questions.quote: They have struck down another milquetoast pro-life regulation, a Louisiana law which would have required abortion clinics to have hospital admitting privileges within 30 miles.

questions.question: Why did Chief Justice Roberts vote with the majority despite previously opposing the same legal position?
questions.audience: constitutional conservative weighing precedent vs. text
questions.answer: Roberts had dissented from the 2016 Texas case that struck down a similar law, but he sided with the four Democrat-appointed justices in 2020 on the basis of stare decisis, treating the prior ruling as binding precedent regardless of his view of its merits.
questions.quote: Even though Roberts previously dissented the 2016 decision, he concurred with Monday's decision on the basis of the “precedent” that the 2016 decision set.

questions.question: Why does Lockwood say strategies built on overturning Roe through precedent are doomed?
questions.answer: Because the Court treats precedent, not the Constitution, as its governing authority, and Roe itself is precedent. Any reasoning chain that respects precedent will leave Roe in place.
questions.quote: So long as its decisions are made on the basis of precedent, *Roe* will never be overturned. *Roe* *is* precedent.

questions.question: What does the failure of a mere regulation imply about the prospects for an outright abortion ban at the Supreme Court?
questions.audience: state legislator considering abolition vs. regulation
questions.answer: If the Court will not even let states regulate which clinics may operate based on safety credentials, there is no realistic basis to expect it would uphold legislation criminalizing abortion as murder. The regulatory path offers no on-ramp to abolition.
questions.quote: If the Supreme Court won't even uphold a law which merely regulates murder, how can we expect it to uphold a law criminalizing abortion altogether?

questions.question: Why does Lockwood argue the Louisiana law was destined to lose on its own terms?
questions.audience: pro-life advocate frustrated with Supreme Court losses
questions.answer: The law conceded the pro-abortion frame by treating abortion as a legitimate medical procedure that simply needed safety regulation. Building a defense from that false premise hollowed out any moral or legal force behind it.
questions.quote: It treated abortion like a medical procedure, then argued from that false premise that it should be regulated in order to keep those getting abortions safe.

questions.question: What course of action does Lockwood prescribe for state officials in response?
questions.audience: state legislator considering abolition vs. regulation
questions.answer: He calls for magistrates to defy the Supreme Court and criminalize abortion as murder, grounding the duty in the Fourteenth Amendment and Amos 5:15 rather than waiting for federal permission.
questions.quote: It is the constitutional and God-given duty of the states to criminalize abortion as murder, as outlined in the 14th Amendment and Amos 5:15.

questions.question: How does the article frame the abolitionist plan in contrast to continued pro-life advocacy?
questions.answer: Lockwood describes abolition as a straightforward biblical mandate: obey God by defying tyrants, establish justice for the vulnerable, and trust God with the outcome rather than tailoring legislation to win Court approval.
questions.quote: Abolitionists, on the other hand, have a simple, feasible, and biblical plan for abolishing abortion: Obey God by defying tyrants! Establish justice for the least.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Develops the same critique that pro-life groups treat the Supreme Court as the final authority instead of defying it.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Defends the constitutional theory of state defiance that Lockwood urges magistrates to adopt.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Expands the case against the kind of regulatory bill that Louisiana passed and the Court struck down.

related.title: WATCH: How the Seven-Pronged Pro-Life Political Strategy Has Failed
related.reason: Catalogs the broader failure pattern that the June Medical loss exemplifies.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Sets out the immediatist alternative Lockwood prescribes once the regulatory path is rejected.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Illustrates the pro-life resistance to the state-defiance strategy this article calls for.
